title: Type Anatomy — The Vocabulary of Letterforms
content: Understanding type anatomy is the foundation of typographic decision-making. Key terms: baseline (line letters sit on), cap height (height of uppercase letters), x-height (height of lowercase letters — critical for readability), ascender (part rising above x-height: b, d, h), descender (part below baseline: g, p, y), counter (enclosed or partially enclosed space within a letter: o, e), serif (small strokes at letter terminals), stem (main vertical stroke), bowl (curved stroke forming a letter's body). The x-height ratio is the most important readability metric — high x-height = more readable at small sizes.

title: Type Classification — Understanding the Major Families
content: Type families and their UI use cases: Serif (Times, Georgia, Garamond) — traditional authority, high readability in print, editorial. Sans-serif (Helvetica, Inter, SF Pro) — modern, neutral, screen-optimised, most UI default. Transitional serif (Baskerville, Georgia) — elegant bridge between old and modern. Slab serif (Rockwell, Clarendon) — strong personality, headlines, display. Monospace (JetBrains Mono, Fira Code) — code, data, tabular numbers. Script/handwritten — personality, signatures, never for body text. Display — headlines only, never body. Variable fonts span multiple classifications and allow weight/width/optical size axes to shift dynamically.

title: Optical vs Mechanical Spacing — Why Type Feels Wrong
content: Mechanical spacing is mathematically equal. Optical spacing is visually equal — and they are not the same. The eye perceives spacing differently depending on letter shapes: round letters (O, C) need to overshoot the baseline and cap height slightly or they appear shorter. Straight-sided letters (H, E) appear wider than round ones even at the same width. This is why optical corrections exist in professional type design. In practice: letters set at mechanical equal spacing will look uneven. Kerning and optical sizing fix this. Never trust pixel measurements alone — trust your eyes. Test type at the actual size and context it will be rendered.

title: Kerning — The Art of Letter Spacing
content: Kerning is the adjustment of space between specific letter pairs. It exists because the shapes of adjacent letters create visual gaps or collisions that equal spacing doesn't account for. Problem pairs to always check: AV, WA, To, Yo, VA, LT, P. (the 'To' combination is the classic kerning test — the T-arm and o create an apparent hole). Kerning types: metrics kerning (uses built-in font pair data — usually good enough), optical kerning (InDesign/Illustrator calculates it — useful for mixed fonts), manual kerning (override pair by pair — for display type and logos only). At body text sizes, good metrics kerning is sufficient. At display sizes (48px+), always manually check critical pairs.

title: Tracking and Leading — Horizontal and Vertical Space
content: Tracking (letter-spacing) affects the overall density and feel of text. Tight tracking = dense, formal, serious. Loose tracking = airy, approachable, elegant (especially at small sizes). Rules: ALL CAPS text always needs increased tracking (+50 to +100 units) — capital letters were designed for mixed-case reading. Body text: don't touch tracking unless fixing a specific problem. Display text: slight negative tracking (-10 to -20) tightens large headlines. Leading (line-height) determines readability. Body text: 1.4–1.6× font size. Display headlines: tighter, 1.1–1.2×. Tight leading on body text is one of the most common readability errors in UI.

title: Type Scale — Building a Systematic Hierarchy
content: A type scale is a set of predetermined type sizes that relate to each other harmonically. Common scales: Major Third (1.25×), Perfect Fourth (1.333×), Golden Ratio (1.618×). Using a scale ensures visual rhythm and prevents arbitrary size choices. Practical scale for UI (Perfect Fourth, base 16px): 12 / 16 / 21 / 28 / 37 / 50 / 67px. Each step is clearly differentiated. The most common error: too many type sizes that are too close together — the difference between 16px and 18px is invisible as hierarchy. Aim for a minimum 1.25× jump between adjacent levels. Tools: typescale.com, Utopia (fluid type scales).

title: Type Alignment — Left, Right, Centre, Justified
content: Alignment is one of the most impactful typographic decisions. Left-aligned: strongest readability — the eye always returns to a predictable left edge. Use for all body text. Right-aligned: creates ragged left edge, harder to read in long form. Use for captions adjacent to images, or right-column data. Centred: weakest for readability. Use sparingly for short headlines, quotes, or ceremonial text. Never for body text. Justified: creates even margins but produces 'rivers' (gaps running through paragraphs) especially at narrow column widths. Avoid in UI. The alignment principle: choose one alignment per content zone and stay consistent. Mixed alignment within a section signals poor design craft.

title: Measure — The Optimal Line Length
content: Measure is the number of characters per line. Optimal reading measure: 45–75 characters (including spaces). Below 45: eye jumps between lines too frequently, causing reading fatigue. Above 75: the eye struggles to find the beginning of the next line. The 66-character average is the golden standard for long-form reading. In UI: cards and sidebars often force narrow measures — use slightly tighter leading to compensate. For mobile (320–375px width), font size and column padding must be tuned together to maintain measure. The measure problem is why full-width body text at large screen sizes is almost always wrong — use max-width containers on paragraphs.

title: Font Pairing — Principles and Strategy
content: Font pairing works on contrast and harmony. Too similar = indistinct. Too different = chaotic. Rules: pair a serif with a sans-serif for a classic combination. Pair fonts with similar x-heights for visual cohesion. Use different weights within the same family before reaching for a second typeface. Classic pairings: Playfair Display + Source Sans Pro, Merriweather + Open Sans, GT Super + Söhne. Functional pairing strategy: headline font (character, personality), body font (neutral, highly readable), monospace for code. Avoid pairing two display fonts or two script fonts. When in doubt: use one typeface family with multiple weights and styles before adding a second font.

title: Variable Fonts — The Future of Type in UI
content: Variable fonts contain an entire type family in a single file, with axes that can be continuously adjusted: weight (100–900), width (condensed to expanded), optical size, slant, and custom axes. Benefits: one file instead of multiple, smooth weight transitions in animations, optical size axis adjusts spacing/details for small vs large rendering. Key variable fonts for UI: Inter (weight + width), Recursive (weight + slant + casual), Fraunces (optical size + softness). In Figma: variable fonts are fully supported — use the number input next to weight to set intermediate values like 337 or 620. Performance win: one variable font file is smaller than 4 separate font files.

title: Type and Accessibility — Contrast, Size, and Legibility
content: WCAG contrast requirements: AA standard requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt+ or 14pt+ bold). AAA requires 7:1. These are minimums — aim higher. But contrast isn't the only legibility factor. Letter spacing, x-height, stroke contrast (thick/thin variation in letterforms), and background texture all affect legibility. High-contrast thin fonts (like Bodoni) can fail legibility tests despite passing contrast ratios due to very thin strokes. For body text accessibility: minimum 16px, avoid fonts with low x-height, ensure adequate leading (1.5× minimum for WCAG AA). Dyslexia-friendly practices: avoid centred and justified text, increase tracking slightly, use fonts with clear letter differentiation (b/d/p/q).

title: Type Rendering — How Screens Affect Typography
content: Screen rendering fundamentally differs from print. Key considerations: hinting (instructions in the font file telling rendering engines how to align letterforms to pixel grids — critical at small sizes), anti-aliasing (smoothing of letterforms — browser CSS: -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased changes the rendering significantly), subpixel rendering (using RGB subpixels to increase apparent resolution — mostly replaced by greyscale rendering on modern displays), retina/HiDPI displays (2× pixel density effectively doubles the resolution available to type — fonts that looked rough at 1× look beautiful at 2×). Always test type rendering on both 1× and 2× displays. Thin hairline fonts look great on retina, terrible on standard displays.

title: Type Personality — How Fonts Communicate Emotion
content: Every typeface carries emotional associations built through decades of use, cultural context, and inherent form. Geometric sans (Futura, Montserrat) — rational, modern, optimistic, tech. Humanist sans (Gill Sans, Myriad) — approachable, friendly, human. Old-style serif (Garamond, Caslon) — classic, authoritative, literary. Modern serif (Didot, Bodoni) — luxury, fashion, high contrast, dramatic. Slab serif (Rockwell, Memphis) — sturdy, confident, vintage, editorial. Script — personal, handcrafted, feminine or casual depending on style. When choosing type: define the emotional territory first, then find a font that naturally lives there. Never choose a font because it 'looks nice' — ask what it says.

title: Typography in Design Systems — Tokens and Scale
content: In a design system, typography is defined as tokens not point sizes. Token structure: font-family (base, mono, display), font-size (xs/sm/md/lg/xl/2xl + px values), font-weight (regular/medium/semibold/bold), line-height (tight/normal/relaxed + unitless values), letter-spacing (tight/normal/wide + em values). Semantic naming separates structure from value: body-lg-regular not font-size-18-weight-400. This allows theming without touching components. In Figma: use text styles (pre-variables) or type variables (new) to encode the full token set. Bind Figma type styles to code tokens via design tokens plugins. A mature type system has 6–12 type styles, not 40+.
